video_title: Conservation of energy (part 1) | AP Physics | Khan Academy

segments.text: We have a system of two blocks connected via a string that goes over a pulley and one
of the blocks is connected to a spring.
Imagine the spring is relaxed right now.
What would happen if we were to release the block or gravity pulls on it and as a result,
the spring will now get stretched to some extent and then the blocks will again come to a
rest and then the spring might pull it back.
But let's not worry about that.
Let's consider this to be our final state and this is our initial state.
So the question now is what happens or how does the mechanical energy of the system change
when we go from this initial to final state.
But which system are we dealing with?
We're going to consider two scenarios.
In the first scenario, we're going to consider the two block spring system.
So how does its mechanical energy change?
And in the second scenario, we're going to consider the two blocks spring and the earth
as our system.
So how does the mechanical energy of this system change?
That's what we're trying to figure out in this video.
So let's begin.
How do we do this?
Well, since we are interested in mechanical energy, let's recall that mechanical energy
is the sum of the kinetic energy of all the particles of the system,
plus the potential energy of the system.
Okay, but how do you predict how the mechanical energy changes?
Well, that depends upon the work done on the system.
If a positive work is done on the system, then it increases the mechanical energy of the system.
But if there's a negative work done on the system,
it reduces the mechanical energy of the system.
So all we now have to think about is what is the work done on these two systems.
But the problem is there are a ton of forces over here.
We have spring force, we have frictional force, we have the tension forces,
we have the force of gravity, and then some of these forces are part of our system.
Others are not, and some are conservative forces.
Others are non-conservative forces.
So the question is, how do we tackle all of this?
So what we're going to do is let's look at a much simpler case,
so we can build a solid intuition, and then we'll come back.
So the simplest case we'll start with is, we're just going to drop a block from some height,
and we know what's going to happen, it's just going to fall down.
So let's consider this as our initial state and the final state to be just before the block
hitting the ground. What if we consider just the block as our system?
The question now is, what happens to the mechanical energy of this system as it goes from here to here?
Well, whenever a system consists of just one particle,
mechanical energy is just the kinetic energy, because for potential energy,
you need at least two particles interacting.
You have only one particle, we're going to model this block as a single particle.
If you have only one particle, this is only kinetic energy.
Okay, so the question is, what happens to the kinetic energy as the block goes from here to here?
Well, we know the answer to that, it's going to increase, right?
But we want to be able to answer this by using the idea of work done,
because that is something that can be applied to even the most complicated systems.
So let's ask ourselves, what is the work done on this system?
Well, which are the forces acting on our system?
There's only a force of gravity acting on the system.
So gravitational force will change the mechanical energy of our system.
Is it a positive or a negative work?
Well, the force of gravity is downwards,
this displacement is also downwards.
So the work done by gravity is positive, and therefore it increases the mechanical energy of the system.
And that's how we'll conclude the mechanical energy increases.
And of course, this makes sense, because we already know,
we already expect the kinetic energy to increase.
So it makes sense, right?
But what if the block was thrown upwards?
Well, now the force of gravity is still downwards, but the displacement is upwards.
Now, gravity does negative work.
This negative work done on our system, and therefore the mechanical energy reduces.
And again, that's what we would expect, right?
As it goes up, the kinetic energy drops.
So that's how we, by knowing the work done on the system,
we can predict what happens to the mechanical energy.
Okay, now let's consider the exact same case, but this time,
let's consider the earth block system.
What happens to its mechanical energy as we go from here to here?
Okay, this time, let me talk about mechanical energy,
we have to consider the kinetic energy of the block.
And the kinetic energy of the earth,
well, but we're going to assume the earth is not moving.
So it's just the kinetic energy of the block.
And we also now have to consider the potential energy of the earth block system,
because they are interacting using a gravitational force,
which is a conservative force.
Whenever particles are interacting with the conservative force,
we have a potential energy assigned to it.
So now, the mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy plus gravitational potential energy.
And so when gravity does work on this block,
when it increases the kinetic energy of the block,
the gravitational potential energy reduces by the exact same amount,
in such a way that the mechanical energy stays the same.
Similarly, if the gravity was to reduce the kinetic energy,
the gravitational potential energy would increase by the same amount.
In other words, since gravity is a conservative force,
it does not affect the mechanical energy of this system.
